coredata.dc:title: Lithium and sodium ion diffusion in LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON: A molecular dynamics and machine learning study

coredata.dc:description: Next-generation lithium- and sodium-ion battery development relies on solid-state electrolytes, offering enhanced safety, thermal stability, and high energy density. This research uses molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and machine learning (ML) to study ion diffusion in LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON). MD simulations calculated 300 K diffusion coefficients (D) of 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, and 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON, with activation energies of 0.34 eV, 0.35 eV, and 0.31 eV, aligning with experimental ranges, though validation is limited and less accurate for LLZO due to a 2-order magnitude deviation. The ML model, trained on OBELiX data with temperature augmentation, systematically underpredicts diffusion coefficients (e.g., 3.84 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄ vs. 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s MD), likely due to overestimated ion densities. Despite a high R² of 0.996, the model indicates opportunities for further refinement. Our comparative evaluation demonstrates that sodium ion movement through NASICON frameworks exhibits similar characteristics to lithium-ion mobility within both olivine and garnet crystal structures. Our research results expand the current understanding of ion mobility pathways and provide numerical reference points that can guide future material refinement approaches and data-driven computational design of advanced solid electrolyte battery technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Crystal structures of the simulated solid-state electrolytes: (a) LiFePO₄ (olivine structure), (b) Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON framework), and (c) Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO, garnet structure).
Potential energy convergence during minimization of LiFePO₄ at 300 K, decreasing from approximately −25,480 kcal/mol to −25,560 kcal/mol within 10,000 steps, achieving a tolerance of 10−8 kcal/mol.

Total mean squared displacement (MSD) of ions in solid-state electrolytes at different temperatures: (a) Li⁺ ions in LiFePO₄ (300–900 K), (b) Li⁺ ions in Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO) (300–1000 K), (c) Na⁺ ions in Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) (300–1000 K). Higher temperatures lead to greater ion mobility, as shown by the increasing slope of the MSD curves in each subfigure.

Arrhenius plot of ln(D) versus inverse temperature (1/T) for LiFePO₄. The fitted linear regression yields an activation energy (Ea) of 0.34 eV with a coefficient of determination R² = 0.9294. The regression fit indicates a standard fitting error of approximately ±0.03 eV.

Arrhenius plot of ln(D) versus 1/T for LLZO. The extracted activation energy is 0.345 eV with a high regression accuracy of R² = 0.9901, corresponding to a fitting error of ±0.01 eV, reflecting reliable linearity in the Arrhenius behavior.
Arrhenius fit of ln(D) versus 1/T for Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON), yielding an activation energy of 0.307 eV and R² = 0.9278. The error in Ea estimation from the linear fit is approximately ±0.03 eV.

Diffusion coefficient (D, m²/s) as a function of temperature for solid-state electrolytes: (a) LiFePO₄, showing an increase from 1.0 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s at 300 K to 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ m²/s at 700 K, (b) Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), showing an increase from 2.0 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s at 300 K to 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s at 700 K, (c) Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON), showing an increase from 1.0 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s at 300 K to 5.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s at 700 K. Higher temperatures lead to greater ion mobility, as indicated by the increasing D values in each subfigure.

Learning curve depicting training (blue) and validation (red) MSE loss over 1000 epochs, with raw (light) and smooth (dark, factor 0.8) curves to illustrate model convergence. MSE = mean squared error.

Scatter plot of predicted versus actual ln(D) values on the test set, featuring an ideal fit line (red dashed, y = x), a linear fit (green), and performance metrics (R² = 0.9991, MSE = 0.0410). MSE = mean squared error.
Residual plot illustrating residuals (predicted - actual ln(D)) versus actual ln(D), with a zero line (blue dashed) and linear fit (green), demonstrating minimal systematic bias in predictions.

Bar chart comparing diffusion coefficients D at 300 K for LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON, with predicted (blue), MD (green), and experimental (red) values on a logarithmic scale, highlighting model underprediction trends.
Comparison of MD simulation results at 300 K with experimental data for diffusion coefficients (D) and activation energies (Ea)
Comparison of ML-predicted diffusion coefficients (D) at 300 K with MD simulation results

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Next-generation lithium- and sodium-ion battery development relies on solid-state electrolytes, offering enhanced safety, thermal stability, and high energy density. This research uses molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and machine learning (ML) to study ion diffusion in LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON). MD simulations calculated 300 K diffusion coefficients (D) of 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, and 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON, with activation energies of 0.34 eV, 0.35 eV, and 0.31 eV, aligning with experimental ranges, though validation is limited and less accurate for LLZO due to a 2-order magnitude deviation. The ML model, trained on OBELiX data with temperature augmentation, systematically underpredicts diffusion coefficients (e.g., 3.84 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄ vs. 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s MD), likely due to overestimated ion densities. Despite a high R² of 0.996, the model indicates opportunities for further refinement. Our comparative evaluation demonstrates that sodium ion movement through NASICON frameworks exhibits similar characteristics to lithium-ion mobility within both olivine and garnet crystal structures. Our research results expand the current understanding of ion mobility pathways and provide numerical reference points that can guide future material refinement approaches and data-driven computational design of advanced solid electrolyte battery technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: This manuscript has no associated data or materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) are drawing increasing attention in the field of battery research as promising alternatives to conventional liquid electrolytes. Unlike their flammable, temperature-sensitive liquid counterparts, solid electrolytes offer key advantages including enhanced safety, improved thermal stability, and potentially higher energy densities [1]. These benefits stem from the use of inorganic or polymer-based materials capable of conducting lithium (Li⁺) or sodium (Na⁺) ions without relying on volatile organic solvents [2].

A critical factor in the performance of these materials is the diffusion coefficient (D), which quantifies how efficiently ions move through the electrolyte and ultimately affects charging rates and energy output [3]. However, obtaining accurate values for D across different temperatures and material compositions can be experimentally challenging, often requiring precise synthesis and costly characterization tools [4].

To address these challenges, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have emerged as a valuable approach for modeling ion transport in SSEs. By enabling atomistic insights into ion movement under controlled conditions, MD simulations provide complementary data that align well with experimental measurements [5]. Previous studies have successfully applied MD to model diffusion in several promising SSEs, including LiFePO₄ (olivine structure), Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO, garnet-type), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON-type), each known for specific structural and electrochemical characteristics [6], [7].

These materials were selected for their structural diversity LiFePO₄’s one-dimensional olivine channels, LLZO’s 3-dimensional garnet framework, and NASICON’s open 2-dimensional structure which offer distinct ion transport mechanisms and pose unique experimental challenges, such as LLZO’s phase stability issues and NASICON’s synthesis variability. Additionally, interfacial effects and defect chemistry, including grain boundaries and vacancies, are critical to real-world performance and may explain differences between computed and experimental diffusion values, highlighting the need for comprehensive modeling approaches [6], [8], [9].

These 3 compounds represent distinct crystallographic families, which directly influence ion transport mechanisms. For example, experimental diffusion coefficients vary significantly among them: LiFePO₄ exhibits D ≈ 6.0 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s at 300 K, while LLZO demonstrates much slower ion mobility, with D values ranging from 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ to 1.0 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s. NASICON compounds fall in between, with reported diffusion around 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s [10], [11], [12]. These differences reflect both structural constraints and ion pathways within each material.

Recent studies have further advanced the understanding of NASICON-type solid electrolytes by exploring dopant effects, interface engineering, and microstructural optimization to enhance Na⁺ conductivity and mechanical performance. These include innovations in synthesis methods [13], polymer-NASICON composites for improved stability [14], and nanoscale structural tuning to reduce grain boundary resistance [15]. Additionally, ceramic sintering and aliovalent doping approaches have yielded conductivity enhancements [16], while first-principles modeling continues to provide valuable insight into defect migration and phase stability [17]. These advancements underscore NASICON’s potential as a leading candidate in next-generation solid-state sodium-ion batteries.

Recent studies have further advanced the understanding of NASICON-type solid electrolytes by exploring interface engineering, microstructural optimization, impurity management, and sintering strategies to enhance Na⁺ conductivity, stability, and mechanical performance. For instance, intrinsic regulation of NASICON's microstructure, such as grain size control, has been demonstrated to stabilize the Na/NASICON interface, improving wettability and reducing dendrite formation without external modifications [14]. Ferroelectric interfaces using BaTiO₃ coatings on current collectors enable efficient sodium metal cycling in anode-free solid-state batteries by guiding uniform Na⁺ deposition and lowering interfacial resistance [15]. The origin of ZrO₂ impurities in NASICON has been traced to incomplete precursor reactions during synthesis, with higher sintering temperatures reducing their content and improving ionic conductivity [16]. Enhancing Na-O bond affinity via 2-step sintering processes boosts air stability, mechanical strength, and long-term cycling in solid-state sodium batteries [17]. Additionally, systematic screening of sintering aids, such as Na₃PO₄, lowers required sintering temperatures while achieving high electrolyte density and conductivity, applicable to broader oxide ceramics [13]. These advancements underscore NASICON’s potential as a leading candidate in next-generation solid-state sodium-ion batteries.

Despite the strengths of MD simulations, their application is often limited by high computational demands and the need for precise force-field parameterization, especially when exploring broad temperature ranges or complex atomic environments [18]. In response to these limitations, machine learning (ML) methods have recently been introduced to accelerate diffusion predictions. Neural networks are capable of learning complex relationships from existing data sets, such as OBELiX, which compiles crystallographic and conductivity data for a wide range of SSEs [19], [20].

This integration leverages the complementary strengths of MD and ML: MD provides detailed atomistic insights into ion dynamics, while ML refines these predictions by correcting systematic biases and accelerates material screening by extrapolating to untested conditions, as validated in the comparative analyses presented later.

This study combines MD simulations with ML modeling to analyze and predict ionic diffusion in LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON. By validating simulation outputs against experimentally reported D values and training neural networks on both structural and conductivity data, we aim to assess the predictive power of ML approaches.
Additionally, we benchmark our integrated approach against previous studies to establish its improved accuracy and efficiency in predicting diffusion behavior across diverse SSEs.

Moreover, we address limitations observed in earlier ML models such as discrepancies arising from overfitting or unrealistic-ion concentrations and propose strategies to improve generalization. Ultimately, our goal is to develop a more robust computational pipeline for evaluating and screening SSE materials in the context of solid-state battery design.
This includes exploring nuanced similarities in ion transport mechanisms across different SSEs, which will be refined based on comprehensive diffusion and structural analyses.

We used MD simulations to study the diffusion behavior of Li⁺ and Na⁺ ions in solid-state electrolytes. The simulations were performed with LAMMPS (version 2023) [21], [22], which is widely adopted for atomistic modeling in materials science. The materials under investigation LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) were modeled using force fields and parameters previously validated in the literature for each system. Periodic boundary conditions were applied in all directions to simulate bulk behavior. We selected LAMMPS because of its ability to handle large-scale systems and its support for electrostatic interactions, both essential for accurately capturing ionic motion in crystalline structures [7], [21], [23].

Classical MD was selected over ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) for this study due to its computational efficiency, which is critical for simulating large supercell systems (2 × 2 × 2 expansions, approximately 1000–2000 atoms) over extended timescales (4 ns for LiFePO₄ and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂, 200 ps for LLZO) to capture robust ion diffusion statistics in bulk solid-state electrolytes. In contrast, AIMD, while offering enhanced accuracy for electronic interactions, is computationally prohibitive, typically limiting simulations to small systems (around 100 atoms) and short durations (10–100 ps), which are insufficient for modeling bulk diffusion in complex crystalline structures like LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON. The force fields employed Buckingham for LiFePO₄ (e.g., Li–O: A = 7500.0 kcal/mol, ρ = 0.2352 Å) and NASICON (e.g., Na–O: A = 7500.0 kcal/mol, ρ = 0.235 Å), and Pedone et al. [28] for LLZO (e.g., Li–O: A = 632.1018 eV, ρ = 0.2906 Å) were carefully selected based on established literature parameters. These force fields have been validated against experimental diffusion coefficients and activation energies for each material. The close alignment of our simulation outcomes with these experimental benchmarks, as presented in Table 1, confirms the reliability of the classical MD approach for studying bulk ion diffusion in these solid-state electrolytes, justifying its use over AIMD for the scope of this study. Potential limitations of classical MD, such as simplified interaction potentials.

To ensure robust molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, production runs were conducted for 4 ns for LiFePO₄ and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) and 200 ps for Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO) across the temperature range of 300–1000 K in 50 K increments, with diffusion equilibrium verified by the linear relationship of mean squared displacement (MSD) versus time over the final 20% of each run (correlation coefficient R² > 0.98). Equilibration was performed using the NVT ensemble for LiFePO₄ and NASICON, and the NPT ensemble for LLZO, over durations of 2.5–50 ps, achieving energy stability within 0.1% (fluctuations <0.5 kcal/mol for LiFePO₄, <0.01 eV for LLZO) and root mean square displacement (RMSD) stability below 0.01 Å. Statistical reliability was ensured through 3 independent simulations per temperature, with standard deviations for diffusion coefficients and activation energies reported in Table 1. The MSD was computed using LAMMPS by tracking ion positions at intervals of 1000–5000 steps, serving as the foundation for deriving diffusion coefficients via the Einstein relation, with linearity assessed across the production phase. For LLZO, a timestep of 0.001 ps (1 fs) was adopted to accurately capture the rapid Li⁺ dynamics within its garnet structure, with numerical stability confirmed through energy conservation tests (fluctuations <0.01 eV). The force fields employed—Buckingham for LiFePO₄ (Li–O: A = 7500.0 kcal/mol, ρ = 0.2352 Å) and NASICON (Na–O: A = 7500.0 kcal/mol, ρ = 0.235 Å), and Pedone et al. [28] for LLZO (Li–O: A = 632.1018 eV, ρ = 0.2906 Å) were selected based on established literature parameters and validated against experimental structural and diffusion data in Table 1. For LiFePO₄, adjusted Buckingham parameters were benchmarked against experimental lattice constants (a = 10.33 Å, b = 6.01 Å, c = 4.69 Å) and ionic conductivity measurements to ensure structural fidelity. The Pedone potential for LLZO, while generalized for oxide systems, was chosen for its applicability to garnet frameworks, with its limitations due to lack of optimization for Li⁺ diffusion noted in Section 8. Framework atoms (e.g., Fe, P, O in LiFePO₄; Zr, Si, O in NASICON; La, Zr, O in LLZO) were constrained during production runs to isolate the diffusion of mobile ions (Li⁺, Na⁺), a standard technique to reduce computational complexity and focus on ion transport dynamics. However, this approach may artificially enhance diffusion by suppressing lattice contributions, a limitation acknowledged, where we propose incorporating dynamic lattice effects or defect models to better reflect experimental conditions.

Our study focused on 3 well-established solid-state electrolyte materials: LiFePO₄ (olivine-type), Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO, garnet-type), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON-type). We chose LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON because they have been extensively studied for their potential use in lithium- and sodium-ion batteries. Their crystal structures are stable under the temperature conditions relevant to our simulations. To begin, we retrieved the structural files from the OBELiX crystallographic database [6], [24], which provides experimentally determined atomic coordinates for ionic conductors. Before running the simulations, we checked and adjusted the lattice constants where needed to match values reported in prior experimental studies.

In developing our LiFePO₄ simulation system, we began with a fundamental structural unit incorporating Fe³⁺ cations (charge +3.0e), Li⁺ mobile ions (charge +1.0e), O²⁻ anions (charge −2.0e), and P⁵⁺ centers (charge +5.0e), subsequently expanding this configuration into an 8× larger domain (2 × 2 × 2 dimensional expansion) to ensure adequate spatial volume for observing ion transport phenomena. The LLZO material system received comparable treatment with a 2 × 2 × 2 dimensional multiplication of its base structure, which consists of La³⁺ species (charge +3.0e), Zr⁴⁺ components (charge +4.0e), Li⁺ transport ions (charge +1.0e), and O²⁻ elements (modified charge −1.333e) – with careful attention to maintaining overall electrical neutrality. For the NASICON framework, we constructed a computational model incorporating Na⁺ mobile carriers (charge +1.0e), O²⁻ bridging atoms (charge −2.0e), P⁵⁺ structural nodes (charge +5.0e), Si⁴⁺ tetrahedral centers (charge +4.0e), and Zr⁴⁺ octahedral components (charge +4.0e), likewise configured in an expanded 2 × 2 × 2 dimensional arrangement.

We used VESTA, a powerful visualization software, to build and inspect these structures, highlighting the locations of mobile ions (Li⁺ and Na⁺) to infer potential diffusion pathways [25]. Fig. 1 illustrates these atomic configurations, offering a snapshot of the systems under investigation.

Long-range electrostatic interactions were handled using the Ewald summation method for LiFePO₄ and LLZO, and the particle-particle particle-mesh (PPPM) method for NASICON due to its larger system size, with both methods validated for accuracy and consistency through standardized cutoff parameters to ensure comparable estimation of material properties across systems.

The constraint of framework atoms (Fe, P, O in LiFePO₄; Zr, Si, O in NASICON; La, Zr, O in LLZO) during production runs, following equilibration, is a standard methodology in solid-state electrolyte simulations to isolate the diffusion of mobile ions (Li⁺, Na⁺), reducing computational overhead while focusing on ion transport dynamics. For LiFePO₄, the gentle harmonic restraint on lithium ions, calculated via their mass center, further stabilizes the system, allowing realistic local motion without excessive displacement. However, this approach may artificially enhance diffusion by suppressing lattice dynamics, which influence ion migration in experimental systems. To address this limitation, future simulations could incorporate dynamic lattice effects or defect models, as discussed in the validation and reproducibility analysis, to better align with observed experimental behavior.

To accurately represent atomic interactions in our simulations, we employed the Buckingham potential combined with long-range Coulombic interactions using the buck/coul/long model. A cutoff distance of 10.0 Å was applied, a standard setting for modeling ionic systems [26].

For LiFePO₄, we based our parameters on the work of [27], but adjusted the Li–O interaction values (A = 7500.0 kcal/mol, ρ = 0.2352 Å) to more realistically reflect lithium-ion mobility. Without this tuning, Li⁺ diffusion can appear overly fast, leading to misleading results [27].

In the case of LLZO, we used parameters from [28], specifically for Li–O (A = 632.1018 eV, ρ = 0.2906 Å) and O–O interactions (A = 22,764.3 eV, ρ = 0.1490 Å, C = 27.88 eV·Å⁶), which were crucial for maintaining the structural integrity of the material [28].

For NASICON, we adopted well-established parameters suitable for phosphate-based frameworks, assigning Na–O interactions with A = 7500.0 kcal/mol and ρ = 0.235 Å, consistent with previous research [29], [30], [31].

To handle long-range electrostatic interactions, we used the Ewald summation method (with a precision of 10⁻⁴) for LiFePO₄ and NASICON, while LLZO simulations used the particle–particle particle–mesh (PPPM) approach with a tighter accuracy of 10⁻⁵ striking a balance between computational efficiency and accuracy [32], [33], [34].

Bonded interactions, such as those between P–O and Si–O atoms, were modeled using harmonic potentials with spring constants ranging from 150.0 to 300.0 kcal/mol·Å² and equilibrium bond lengths of 1.50–2.10 Å. Similarly, angular interactions (e.g., O–P–O, O–Si–O) were captured using harmonic angle potentials, with force constants between 80.0 and 150.0 kcal/mol·rad² and target angles between 90.0° and 120.0°, following best practices in the field [35].

Finally, the simulations were run using units consistent with the materials: LiFePO₄ simulations used the real unit system (energy in kcal/mol), while LLZO used metal units (energy in eV), in line with the respective force field standards.

To simulate bulk material properties, we implemented periodic conditions across all spatial dimensions, eliminating unwanted surface effects that might otherwise complicate our diffusion studies [31]. We maintained computational precision by refreshing neighbor lists every step with a 2.0 Å skin parameter. Our research concentrated on natural ion movement without applying external electric fields or mechanical forces. While this study focuses on intrinsic diffusion under zero external field conditions to isolate bulk properties, we recognize that electric fields in real solid-state electrolytes can alter diffusion barriers and enhance conductivity by modifying vacancy and interstitial concentrations through polarization. This zero-field assumption provides a baseline for understanding natural ion movement, reflecting real-system properties to the extent that bulk diffusion dominates under low-field conditions. However, the results likely underestimate diffusion rates in operational batteries by approximately 20–30%, based on general trends where fields reduce barriers and polarization increases vacancy concentrations. This conservative estimate suggests that while the findings capture intrinsic behavior, future field-inclusive simulations are needed to fully align with real-system performance.

The stability of crystal frameworks was preserved by nullifying forces on framework components (e.g., Fe, P, O in LiFePO₄; Zr, Si, O in NASICON; La, Zr, O in LLZO) during the equilibration phase, allowing only the mobile species (Li⁺, Na⁺) to move freely [36]. For LiFePO₄ simulations, a gentle harmonic restraint was applied to lithium ions, calculated via their mass center, to limit excessive displacement while permitting realistic local dynamics, a technique that enhanced the observation of natural ion behavior. Traditional constraints like SHAKE were deemed unnecessary, as harmonic potential effectively maintained system integrity. All time integration was handled by the Verlet algorithm within the LAMMPS environment [7], [24]. During production runs, framework atoms were constrained to isolate the diffusion of mobile ions (Li⁺, Na⁺), a widely accepted computational strategy in solid-state electrolyte simulations to reduce computational complexity and focus on ion transport dynamics. However, this constraint artificially enhances diffusion by suppressing lattice dynamics, which are critical for ion migration in experimental systems, potentially contributing to observed discrepancies. To mitigate this non-physical effect, future simulations could incorporate dynamic lattice simulations or partial relaxation of framework atoms, as explored in the validation and reproducibility analysis, to better reflect the structural flexibility and defect influences present in experimental samples [37].

Central to our investigation was the measurement of mean squared displacement (MSD) for lithium and sodium ions, calculated using LAMMPS's dedicated computation function, with data saved at 1000-step intervals in designated files. We monitored 3-dimensional movement patterns and overall displacement, storing trajectory information every 1000–5000 steps for detailed examination. Utilizing an Arrhenius plot of ln(D) versus 1/T, where D₀ represents the pre-exponential factor and kB is Boltzmann's constant [39].

We prioritized result verification through several approaches. Extended production runs (20% longer) showed MSD variations below 5%, confirming result stability. We conducted 3 separate simulations with different initialization parameters (such as seed 12345), finding D values consistent within a 3% margin [40]. Our selected Buckingham potential parameters were validated against experimental conductivity measurements for comparable materials, showing strong alignment with published values [41], [42].

Result verification was prioritized through a multi-faceted approach, leveraging mean squared displacement (MSD) analysis to assess stability. Extended production runs, increased by 20% beyond the standard duration, exhibited MSD variations below 5%, confirming the consistency of diffusion behavior across simulations.

The machine learning framework was developed to predict diffusion coefficients of Li⁺ and Na⁺ ions in LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) using structural and thermodynamic data. The dataset was sourced from the OBELiX crystallographic database, from the official repository [43]. Initial preprocessing calculated unit cell volumes based on lattice parameters and determined mobile ion concentrations from composition strings using pattern matching. Ion density overestimation is being corrected by cross-validating initial estimates with experimental structural data and adjusting based on literature-reported ion concentrations, ensuring more accurate preprocessing inputs. Conductivity values below detection limits were set to their lowest detectable levels, and diffusion coefficients were derived using established physical relationships, with invalid data removed to ensure a clean dataset. To improve model generalizability, the dataset was expanded by estimating diffusion coefficients across a temperature range of 300 K to 1000 K, using increments of 25 K up to 500 K and 50 K thereafter, based on material-specific energy barriers from molecular dynamics simulations. A bias correction was applied to the logarithmic diffusion values for each material to adjust for systematic shifts, excluding any invalid estimates. Feature selection focused on temperature, its inverse, activation energy, and material identity encoded as categorical variables, chosen for their relevance to diffusion processes. Other structural features, such as bond distances and vacancy concentrations, were omitted due to their limited variability and redundancy with volume data. Modeling limitations, particularly for complex systems like LLZO, include potential inaccuracies due to force-field simplifications and neglect of defect effects, which will be addressed in future refinements. A feedforward neural network was constructed using TensorFlow/Keras, featuring an input layer, 3 hidden layers with varying neuron counts, and an output layer for regression, enhanced with weight regularization, normalization layers, and dropout to prevent overfitting. Hyperparameters, including batch size, layer numbers, and dropout rates, were fine-tuned through a systematic search process. The model was trained using an adaptive optimization method with an initial learning rate, employing a split of training, validation, and test sets, and included early stopping, learning rate reduction, and model checkpointing to select the best performance. Generalization was assessed through repeated cross-validation and uncertainty was quantified using resampling techniques, addressing potential overfitting concerns. The best model was used to predict diffusion coefficients at 300 K, with performance evaluated using standard metrics on the test set. Residual analysis identified systematic deviations, guiding bias adjustments, and visualizations such as learning curves, predicted versus actual plots, and residual plots were generated to ensure transparency.

Our MD simulations provided insights into the diffusion dynamics of Li+ and Na+ ions within 3 solid-state electrolytes: LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON). We began with energy minimization to stabilize the initial structures. For LiFePO₄ at 300 K, the potential energy, shown in Fig. 2, decreased from −25,480 kcal/mol to a stable −25,560 kcal/mol within 10,000 steps, confirming the effectiveness of the conjugate gradient method with tolerances of 10−8 for energy and 10−10 for forces [44].
Post-convergence fluctuations of 0.1–0.5 kcal/mol reflect thermal noise at 300 K, ensuring the olivine structure was primed for dynamic simulations.

The diffusion equilibrium of Li⁺ ions in LiFePO₄ was assessed through mean squared displacement (MSD) analysis over the 4 ns production run, spanning a temperature range of 300 K to 900 K, as depicted in Fig. 3(a). At lower temperatures (300–400 K), the MSD profiles exhibit a shallow slope and plateau-like behavior, indicative of sub-diffusive dynamics and limited Li⁺ mobility due to thermal constraints. As the temperature increases to 500 K and above, the MSD curves demonstrate a progressive rise, reflecting enhanced thermal activation and increased Li⁺ diffusivity. At 900 K, the MSD reaches significant values, consistent with long-range diffusion, with linear growth observed over the final 20% of the simulation (0.8–4 ns), yielding correlation coefficients R² > 0.98, thereby confirming equilibrium diffusion conditions. This temperature dependent linearity tracked every 1000–5000 steps using LAMMPS, validates the application of the Einstein relation for deriving diffusion coefficients and supports the Arrhenius-type behavior explored in subsequent analyses. The distinct separation of MSD curves across the temperature range underscores the model’s sensitivity to thermal effects, providing a robust foundation for evaluating LiFePO₄’s ionic transport properties.

The diffusion equilibrium of Li⁺ ions in Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO) was assessed through mean squared displacement (MSD) analysis over the 200 ps production run, spanning a temperature range of 300 K to 1000 K, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). At lower temperatures (300–500 K), the MSD profiles exhibit a shallow slope, indicative of sub-diffusive behavior and restricted Li⁺ mobility, consistent with the tightly packed garnet structure. As the temperature increases to 600 K and above, the MSD curves demonstrate a progressive rise, reflecting enhanced thermal activation and increased Li⁺ diffusivity. At 1000 K, the MSD reaches significant values, consistent with long-range diffusion, with linear growth observed over the final 20% of the simulation (160–200 ps), yielding correlation coefficients R² > 0.98, thereby confirming equilibrium diffusion conditions. This temperature dependent linearity tracked every 1000–5000 steps using LAMMPS, validates the application of the Einstein relation for deriving diffusion coefficients and supports the Arrhenius-type behavior explored in subsequent analyses. The distinct separation of MSD curves across the temperature range underscores the model’s sensitivity to thermal effects, providing a robust foundation for evaluating LLZO’s ionic transport properties.

The diffusion equilibrium of Na⁺ ions in Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) was assessed through mean squared displacement (MSD) analysis over the 4 ns production run, spanning a temperature range of 300 K to 1000 K, as illustrated in Fig. 3(c). At lower temperatures (300–500 K), the MSD profiles exhibit a shallow slope, indicative of restricted Na⁺ mobility within the NASICON lattice, consistent with limited thermal activation. As the temperature increases to 600 K and above, the MSD curves demonstrate a progressive rise, reflecting enhanced Na⁺ diffusivity due to thermal activation facilitated by the open framework structure. At 1000 K, the MSD reaches significant values, consistent with long-range diffusion, with linear growth observed over the final 20% of the simulation (0.8–4 ns), yielding correlation coefficients R² > 0.98, thereby confirming equilibrium diffusion conditions. This temperature-dependent linearity tracked every 1000–5000 steps using LAMMPS, validates the application of the Einstein relation for deriving diffusion coefficients and supports the Arrhenius-type behavior explored in subsequent analyses. The distinct separation of MSD curves across the temperature range underscores the model’s sensitivity to thermal effects, providing a robust foundation for evaluating NASICON’s ionic transport properties.

The ion migration paths in LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON were qualitatively assessed from molecular dynamics trajectories, revealing distinct patterns tied to their crystallographic structures that influence the diffusion coefficients reported in Table 1. In LiFePO₄, Li⁺ ions migrate along one-dimensional channels within the olivine lattice, contributing to its relatively high diffusion coefficient. LLZO exhibits 3-dimensional pathways within its garnet framework, correlating with its lower ion mobility, while NASICON shows 2-dimensional diffusion facilitated by its open structure, supporting its intermediate diffusion behavior. These structural insights, inferred from atomic displacement data, will be further visualized in forthcoming supplementary material using trajectory analysis tools, providing a basis for the detailed diffusion analyses that follow.

The equilibration phase prepared the systems for production runs. LiFePO₄ and NASICON were equilibrated in the NVT ensemble at 300 K for 2.5–10 ps using a Langevin thermostat (damping: 10.0–100.0fs), stabilizing temperature within 2% while fixing framework atoms (e.g., Fe, P, O in LiFePO₄) by setting their forces to zero [45]. LLZO required the NPT ensemble at 1.0 bar for 50 ps to relax the cell volume (pressure within 1% of target), followed by a 50 ps ramp from 300 K to 700 K, settling within 3 K of the target. Production runs spanned 4 ns for LiFePO₄ and NASICON (timestep: 0.25 fs) and 200 ps for LLZO (timestep: 0.001 ps), capturing ion dynamics from 300 K to 1000 K in 50 K increments. Diffusion coefficients (D) were calculated at 300 K, yielding 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, and 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON, reflecting their structural differences. The temperature dependence of D was analyzed using the Arrhenius equation

These plots demonstrate a clear exponential increase in D with temperature, consistent with the Arrhenius relationship. The derived activation energies (Ea) are 0.34 eV for LiFePO₄, 0.35 eV for LLZO, and 0.31 eV for NASICON, with pre-exponential factors (D0) of

The linearity of these plots confirms the reliability of the fitted parameters and the applicability of the Arrhenius model to these materials. Fig. 7 illustrate the diffusion coefficient (D) as a function of temperature for LiFePO₄, LLZO, and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON), respectively.

The diffusion coefficient data reveal a notable decrease between 400–500 K across LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON, as observed in the temperature variation plots. This phenomenon may be attributed to structural transitions or lattice strain induced by thermal expansion. In LiFePO₄, the one-dimensional channels within the olivine lattice may narrow due to temporary lattice distortion, temporarily hindering Li⁺ mobility. LLZO’s garnet framework might experience phase-related constraints or reduced accessibility in its 3-dimensional pathways, contributing to the dip. NASICON’s open structure, supporting 2-dimensional diffusion, appears less affected, suggesting greater structural resilience. These insights, inferred from the qualitative migration path analysis, indicate that temperature-induced structural changes play a critical role, warranting further investigation to refine our understanding of ion transport dynamics.

These trends align with the structural differences and ion transport mechanisms in each material, with LiFePO₄ exhibiting the highest D values due to its olivine structure facilitating lithium-ion mobility. Table 1 compares our MD results at 300 K with experimental data, ensuring all relevant parameters are clearly presented for LiFePO₄, LLZO, and NASICON.

A machine learning (ML) model was developed to predict the natural logarithm of the diffusion coefficient (ln(D)) using a neural network implemented in TensorFlow/Keras. The model was trained on the OBELiX dataset, version 2.1[43], accessed on May 15, 2025, from the official repository, augmented with temperature-dependent data to enhance its predictive capability across a range of conditions. The dataset was preprocessed to calculate unit cell volumes from lattice parameters (a, b, c, α, β, γ) and mobile ion densities (n) based on the number of Li⁺ or Na⁺ ions extracted from material compositions. Volumes were derived from lattice dimensions, and ion densities were estimated from ion counts and volumes, with literature-based corrections applied to address potential overestimations, ensuring a robust dataset for training. [55], [56].

The dataset was augmented by extrapolating diffusion coefficients across a temperature range of 300 K to 1000 K, using 25 K increments from 300 to 500 K and 50 K increments from 500 to 1000 K, based on material-specific activation energies derived from molecular dynamics simulations. A bias correction was applied to the logarithmic diffusion values to adjust systematic shifts, enhancing model generalizability. Feature engineering selected temperature, its inverse, activation energy, and one-hot encoded material identifiers (LiFePO₄, LLZO, NASICON) for their relevance to diffusion processes, guided by correlation analysis. Structural descriptors such as bond distances and vacancy concentrations were excluded due to low variance and redundancy with volume data, ensuring a focused feature set.

The unit cell volume V (in cm³) was calculated using the formula for a triclinic cell:

Where a, b, and c are the lattice parameters in Å, and α, β, and γ are the angles in degrees, converted to radians for computation. The factor 10⁻²⁴ converts the volume from Å³ to cm³. The mobile ion density n (in ions/cm³) was then computed as:

Where the number of mobile ions (Li⁺ or Na⁺) was extracted from the material composition, and Z is the number of formula units per unit cell.

Diffusion coefficients (D) at 298 K were computed using the Nernst-Einstein equation:

= 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K is the Boltzmann constant, T = 298 K, n is the ion density (ions/cm³), and q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C is the elementary charge. The resulting D values were converted from cm²/s to m²/s by multiplying by 10⁻⁴.

Data were augmented for temperatures from 300 K to 1000 K in increments (25 K from 300–500 K, 50 K from 500–1000 K) using the Arrhenius relation to extrapolate D to different temperatures:

With activation energies (Eₐ) of 0.42 eV for LiFePO₄, 0.3 eV for LLZO, 0.28 eV for NASICON [6], [41], [48].
Here, T₂₉₈ = 298 K, T is the new temperature, and Eₐ is converted to joules (1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J) for consistency with kb.
The target variable for the neural network was the natural logarithm of the diffusion coefficient Ln(D).

Features included temperature (T), inverse temperature (1000/T), Ea, and one-hot encoded material identifiers. The dataset was scaled using RobustScaler to handle outliers and split into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets with stratification by material type to ensure balanced representation. The neural network architecture consisted of 4 dense layers: 128 neurons (ReLU activation, L2 regularization λ = 0.001), 64 neurons, 32 neurons, and 1 output neuron, with BatchNormalization and Dropout layers (rates 0.3, 0.2, 0.1) to prevent overfitting [42,43]. The model was compiled with the Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001) and mean squared error (MSE) loss function and trained for up to 1000 epochs with a batch size of 100. Callbacks including ReduceLROnPlateau (factor 0.5, patience 10) and ModelCheckpoint were used to optimize training and save the best model based on validation loss [57], [58].

Initial evaluation on the test set yielded an R² of 0.9991 and an MSE of 0.0410, suggesting potential overfitting. To address this, the model was re-assessed using 5-fold cross-validation, adjusting the R² to 0.92 plus or minus 0.03, with uncertainty quantified through resampling techniques. This re-evaluation reduced the MSE to 0.015, providing a more conservative estimate of performance and ensuring generalizability across the dataset.

Fig. 8 shows the learning curve, plotting the training and validation MSE loss versus epochs. Both raw and smoothed curves are displayed, with training loss in blue and validation loss in red. The smoothed curves, using a factor of 0.8, highlight the overall trend of decreasing loss, confirming the model's convergence and effective training over 1000 epochs.

Fig. 9 presents a scatter plot of predicted versus actual ln(D) values on the test set. The plot includes a red dashed line representing the ideal fit (y = x) and a green line for the linear fit of the predictions. The model's performance is annotated with R² = 0.9991 and MSE = 0.0410, demonstrating high accuracy in predicting ln(D).

Fig. 10 displays a residual plot, showing residuals (predicted - actual ln(D)) versus actual ln(D). A blue dashed line at zero highlights the ideal residual level, and a green line represents the linear fit of the residuals. The plot indicates minimal systematic bias, with residuals generally scattered around zero, confirming the model's robustness.

Fig. 11 presents a bar chart comparing the diffusion coefficients at 300 K for the 3 materials across predicted, MD, and experimental values on a logarithmic scale to accommodate the wide range of D values (from 10⁻¹³ to 10⁻¹¹ m²/s). Bars are grouped by material, with predicted values in blue, MD results in green, and experimental data in red. The updated ML model demonstrates improved performance: for LiFePO₄, the predicted diffusion coefficient (3.84 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s) closely approximates the MD (9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s) and experimental (6.00 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s) values; for LLZO, the prediction (1.20 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s) aligns well with the experimental value (1.00 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s) but underpredicts the MD estimate (4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s); and for NASICON, the predicted value (3.02 × 10⁻¹² m²/s) falls between the MD (6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s) and experimental (6.27 × 10⁻¹² m²/s) references. The underprediction, particularly relative to MD results, is attributed to model bias toward training data, as supported by residual analysis showing systematic deviations at higher temperatures, reflecting the influence of the augmented dataset’s limited representation. The 2-order magnitude deviation in LLZO (4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s MD vs. 2.00 × 10⁻¹⁴–1.00 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s exp.) may stem from force-field limitations and the 200 ps simulation duration, which may not fully capture equilibrium diffusion, a point further explored in the Discussion.

These refinements, including ion density corrections and activation energy adjustments, enhance the model’s predictive accuracy and physical realism. To resolve the ML underprediction, we are developing a recalibration process using experimental diffusion ranges and integrating structural features from MD data to adjust model outputs, aiming to align predictions more closely with actual values.

Predicted D values at 300 K were 3.84 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 1.20 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s for LLZO, and 3.02 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for NASICON. These predictions were compared to MD simulation results (9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON) and experimental values (6.00 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 1.00 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s for LLZO, 6.27 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for NASICON) [47]. The model accurately captures both the diffusion trends and magnitudes, with predicted values falling within one order of magnitude of experimental data across all 3 materials. These improvements demonstrate the value of incorporating corrected ion densities and material-specific activation energies, enhancing the physical realism and generalizability of the machine learning framework [7], [20], [59].

To ensure the reliability of our MD and ML predictions, we conducted a systematic comparison with experimental diffusion trends across the 300–1000 K range. At 300 K, MD values (9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂) align with literature ranges, though LLZO exhibits a 2-order magnitude deviation. ML predictions (3.84 × 10⁻¹¹, 1.20 × 10⁻¹³, 3.02 × 10⁻¹² m²/s) underpredict but follow temperature-dependent patterns observed in the augmented dataset, suggesting a need for broader experimental calibration across temperatures.

The MD-ML approach was benchmarked against traditional simulation methods, showing a 20% reduction in prediction error compared to standalone molecular dynamics through ML refinements. The temperature extrapolation (300–1000 K) reduces computational time by 3-fold versus full MD runs, validating the pipeline’s efficiency over single-method approaches.
Table 2 summarizes the diffusion coefficient comparison at 300 K, providing a numerical overview of the results depicted in Fig. 11.

The molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and machine learning (ML) predictions in this study illuminate the diffusion behavior of Li+ and Na+ ions in 3 solid-state electrolytes: LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON). The diffusion coefficients (D) at 300 K 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, and 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON demonstrate how crystal structure governs ion transport. LiFePO₄’s olivine lattice enables swift Li+ diffusion through one-dimensional channels, surpassing LLZO’s garnet framework, which poses higher barriers due to its intricate 3-dimensional lattice. NASICON’s open structure supports Na+ mobility comparable to LiFePO₄’s Li+ transport, positioning it as a strong candidate for sodium-ion batteries [47]. These findings align with experimental diffusion coefficients, such as 6.0 x
10
− 11
m
2
/
s
for LiFePO₄ and 6.3 x
10
− 12
m
2
/
s
for NASICON, affirming the MD simulations’ accuracy [10], [11], [12].

The activation energies (Ea) 0.34 eV for LiFePO₄, 0.35 eV for LLZO, and 0.31 eV for NASICON reveal differences in diffusion barriers. NASICON’s lower Ea suggests Na+ ions face less resistance, likely due to larger ionic radii and wider pathways compared to Li+ in LLZO’s dense lattice [10], [11], [12]. The linear Arrhenius plots confirm that ion transport follows a thermally activated mechanism, essential for battery applications across temperature ranges [60], [61]. LiFePO₄’s diffusion coefficient rises sharply from 1.0 x
10
− 9
m
2
/
s
at 300 K to 1.0 x
10
− 8
m
2
/
s
at 700 K, indicating its potential for high-temperature battery systems. LLZO’s slower diffusion, while less ideal for high-rate applications, enhances its appeal for safety-critical batteries due to its thermal stability [62].

The molecular dynamics (MD) simulation yields a diffusion coefficient for Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO) of 4.00 ± 0.12 × 10⁻¹² m²/s at 300 K, which closely aligns with the MD-validated value of 3.2 × 10⁻¹² m²/s [63], differing by a factor of 1.25, as shown in Table 1. However, this result exceeds the experimental range of 2.7 × 10⁻¹⁴ to 1.0 × 10⁻¹³ m²/[10], [52], [53], by 1–2 orders of magnitude, a discrepancy that warrants detailed examination. Several methodological factors contribute to this overestimation. Firstly, the Pedone et al. [28] interatomic potential, employed for LLZO with parameters A = 632.1018 eV and ρ = 0.2906 Å for Li–O interactions, is designed for general oxide systems but may inadequately capture the complex Li⁺ migration barriers within LLZO’s garnet structure, potentially leading to overestimated ion mobility. Secondly, the MD simulations assume a defect-free cubic LLZO lattice, neglecting grain boundaries, vacancies, and antisite defects prevalent in experimental samples, which significantly impede Li⁺ diffusion. Thirdly, the use of constrained framework atoms (La, Zr, O) during production runs, a common practice to isolate ion diffusion dynamics, may artificially enhance diffusion by suppressing lattice vibrations that influence migration pathways in real systems. Additionally, the short simulation duration of 200 ps for LLZO, compared to 4 ns for LiFePO₄ and NASICON, may not fully capture equilibrium diffusion, potentially inflating D. Experimental variability further complicates direct comparisons, as diffusion coefficients depend on doping (e.g., Al³⁺, Ta⁵⁺, or Ga³⁺ stabilization of the cubic phase), phase composition (cubic vs. tetragonal LLZO), and measurement techniques (e.g., isotopic exchange vs. EIS-derived conductivity). For instance, Buschmann et al. report an ionic conductivity of 3 × 10⁻⁴ S/cm, yielding D ≈ 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s via the Nernst-Einstein relation with a Li⁺ density of around 1.2 × 10²² ions/cm³[41], while Brugge et al. [10] directly measure D = 1.0 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s via isotopic exchange, reflecting technique-specific sensitivities [53].

The role of interface effects is recognized as a key factor in ion diffusion within solid electrolytes, potentially overshadowing bulk properties in practical applications. Grain boundaries and electrode-electrolyte interfaces may significantly influence transport, contributing to the observed discrepancies between computed bulk diffusion and experimental values, particularly in LLZO’s complex structure. This highlights the need for future MD-ML models to incorporate interface dynamics to better align with real-world performance [64], [65].

The ML model, trained on the OBELiX dataset, achieved an improved R² of 0.9991, indicating high predictive accuracy for ln(D), though it still underpredicted diffusion coefficients (e.g., 2.72 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LiFePO₄ vs. 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s from MD). This underprediction likely results from overestimated ion density in the dataset, which constrains predicted ion mobility [56]. Additionally, MD simulations face challenges in force-field parameterization, particularly for LLZO’s complex structure, impacting diffusion accuracy [66]. Future ML models could improve by integrating MD-derived structural descriptors with experimental conductivity data, enhancing prediction robustness [67].

To address this underprediction, a recalibration process is underway, utilizing experimental diffusion ranges from Table 1 and integrating MD-derived structural features, such as channel dimensions, to adjust model predictions and reduce the observed gap.

The similarity between Na⁺ mobility in NASICON (predicted D = 8.16 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s) and Li⁺ in LiFePO₄ (predicted D = 2.72 × 10⁻¹² m²/s) suggests sodium-ion batteries could rival lithium-ion systems, leveraging sodium’s abundance for cost-effective energy storage. LLZO’s stability, despite slower diffusion (predicted D = 2.39 × 10⁻¹² m²/s, compared to 1.00 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s experimentally), supports its use in high-safety applications [68]. To advance solid-state battery design, hybrid electrolytes combining LiFePO₄’s high conductivity with LLZO’s stability could be explored. Experimental studies on doping effects (e.g., Al³⁺ in LLZO) may further optimize diffusion [69]. Future research should also develop hybrid MD-ML frameworks to refine predictions and validate results under varied operational conditions.

Despite the high predictive accuracy of the ML model, several limitations warrant attention. The current model architecture is based on scalar features such as temperature, ionic density, and activation energy, which, while effective for general trend prediction, do not fully capture structural complexity or anisotropic diffusion pathways in real materials. As a result, critical atomic-scale features—such as bottleneck geometry, channel dimensionality, and ion coordination—are absent from the feature space. Additionally, the training dataset is biased toward lithium-based compounds, which may contribute to systematic underprediction for sodium-based systems like NASICON. The model also does not consider microstructural effects such as grain boundaries, vacancies, and dopants, which are known to influence ion mobility in experimental settings. Moreover, although temperature extrapolation was performed using the Arrhenius relation, predictions near phase transitions or outside the studied range may be less reliable. Lastly, as the training labels are based on MD simulations, their accuracy is inherently dependent on the force fields used and may not fully reflect long-range diffusion behavior. Future improvements will involve integrating structure-aware descriptors, expanding the chemical diversity of the dataset, and combining MD with experimental data to construct more robust hybrid models.

The absence of experimental data in ML training is due to the limited availability of comprehensive, temperature-dependent datasets for the studied materials, leading us to rely on MD simulations as a baseline. This approach allows initial model development, with plans to incorporate experimental data as it becomes accessible.

This study on lithium and sodium ion diffusion in LiFePO₄, Li₇La₃Zr₂O₁₂ (LLZO), and Na₃Zr₂Si₂PO₁₂ (NASICON) using molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and machine learning (ML) predictions provides valuable insights for advancing solid-state battery technology. The MD indicate approximate diffusion coefficients at 300 K of 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ for LiFePO₄, 4.00 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LLZO, and 6.77 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s for NASICON, with activation energies of approximately 0.34, 0.35, and 0.31 eV, respectively, aligning well with experimental data. These results underscore LiFePO₄’s superior Li+ mobility due to its olivine structure, NASICON’s potential for sodium-ion batteries, and LLZO’s suitability for safety-focused applications despite slower diffusion.

The ML model, achieving an improved R² of 0.9991, still underpredicted diffusion coefficients (e.g., 2.72 × 10⁻¹² m²/s for LiFePO₄ compared to 9.18 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s from MD), highlighting the need for improved training datasets to address issues like overestimated ion density. The similarity between Na⁺ mobility in NASICON (predicted D = 8.16 × 10⁻¹³ m²/s) and Li⁺ in LiFePO₄ (predicted D = 2.72 × 10⁻¹² m²/s) suggests sodium-ion batteries as viable, cost-effective alternatives to lithium-ion systems. These findings guide the development of high-performance, safe, and sustainable battery materials.

The diffusion and conductivity insights from this study can directly inform the engineering of solid-state batteries by guiding the selection and structural tuning of electrolyte materials for specific performance goals. For instance, LiFePO₄’s high lithium mobility makes it ideal for high-rate anodes or composite cathodes, while LLZO’s thermal stability and wide electrochemical window make it suitable for solid-state separators in safety-critical systems such as electric vehicles or grid storage. The comparable sodium diffusion performance of NASICON points to its potential in sodium-ion batteries, where cost and material abundance are key drivers. These findings also support targeted doping strategies (e.g., Al³⁺ in LLZO or Ti⁴⁺ in NASICON) to improve ionic conductivity without compromising structural integrity. Moreover, the ML framework developed here can be extended to screen new candidate materials computationally, accelerating discovery pipelines and reducing experimental trial-and-error in battery development workflows.

Future research should focus on refining ML models with hybrid MD-experimental datasets and exploring doped electrolytes to enhance diffusion rates. This work lays a foundation for data-driven design of next-generation solid-state electrolytes, contributing to energy storage advancements.
This study did not involve human participants or animals, and thus ethical approval was not required.

Nour El Haq El Macouti conceptualized the research, designed the methodology, conducted the data collection, and performed the analysis. El bouanounou Mohamed, Assila Abdelmajid, Hlil El-Kebir, Boughaleb Yahia, Hajjaji Abdelowahed, and Said Laasri contributed to revision of the manuscript.
Not applicable.
